title: Markovian dynamics of single-rebit open quantum systems with applications to colour perception
authors: Michel Berthier, Gabriel Niebel, Edoardo Provenzi
abstract: This paper investigates the Markovian dynamics of open two-state quantum systems defined over the real numbers (rebits). Two main objectives are pursued. First, we present a comprehensive classification of Markovian rebit quantum channels, i.e. one-parameter semigroups of completely positive, trace-preserving (CPTP) maps acting on the rebit state space. We show that a full characterisation of their action can be achieved and that describing these channels as solutions of the GKSL equation allows us to explicitly identify the associated Lindblad generators and conditions for complete positivity. Second, we present an original application of this classification to colour perception. Using a recent model in which perceived colours arise from Lüders measurements on the rebit state space, we show how chromatic distortion induced by a non-neutral illuminant can be modelled by a Markovian rebit channel that progressively diminishes colour distinguishability. Other types of channels could be used to study colour vision deficiencies. These phenomena are illustrated by simulations on digital images, highlighting the relevance of rebit Markovian dynamics in modelling colour vision.
summary: The paper develops a comprehensive mathematical classification of Markovian open quantum channels acting on two-state systems (rebits). It then applies this theory to color science, demonstrating that the effect of non-neutral light sources causes chromatic distortion by reducing the distinguishability between colors, which is quantified using relative entropy.
main_problem: The paper classifies Markovian dynamics for open two-state quantum systems (rebits) and applies this framework to model how illuminant changes cause chromatic distortion in human color perception.
main_result: A general structure for Markovian rebit channels is derived, and the theory successfully models chromatic distortion by showing that non-neutral illuminants induce a measurable reduction in color distinguishability quantified by quantum relative entropy.
method: The authors use techniques from open quantum system theory, deriving structural properties of CPTP maps (channels) via the GKSL equation and applying these mathematical tools to psycho-physical models involving Lüders measurements.
model_or_system: The physical system is a single-rebit open quantum system whose state evolves under Markovian dynamics. The model uses the Bloch vector representation for rebit states, $ho_s$, and relates channel evolution to established color vision models.
key_observables: Chromatic distinguishability (quantified by relative entropy $R$), the Bloch vector components $(s_1, s_2)$, and the stationary state $\mathbf{v}_{s0}$.
important_parameters: The decay rates ($\alpha_{i,j}$) derived from the Lindblad operators defining the illuminant channel; the Lorentz boost parameter $\gamma_e$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes continuous differentiability of the channel components and models color perception using a rebit state space parameterized by real numbers.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate chromatic distortion caused by specific non-neutral illuminants (orange, green) and show how the level sets of relative entropy change over time, demonstrating reduced distinguishability.
paper_structure: The paper first tackles the abstract mathematical classification of Markovian rebit channels using semigroup properties. It then transitions to applying this theory to color perception, deriving specific channel forms for illuminants and dysfunction models, and finally quantifying distortion via relative entropy.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous quantum information framework (Markovian dynamics) to model complex biological phenomena like color vision, bridging abstract quantum physics with sensory neuroscience.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 4 Dynamics generated by illuminant channels. The time steps are t = i/10, i = 0, . . . , 14, and the parameters of the Lindblad matrix are as follows: α1,1 = 2, α2,2 = 1/3, α3,3 = 1, α1,3 = 1/7, and α2,3 = 1/5 (left), and α1,1 = 1/8, α2,2 = 1/4, α3,3 = 1/10, α1,3 = −1/20, and α2,3 = −1/10 (right).
Fig. 5 The chromatic distortion induced by an illuminant channel. The weakly saturated orange illuminant is the stationary state (1/3, 0.2), α1,1 = 0.1, α2,2 = 0.25, and α3,3 = 0.15.
Fig. 6 The chromatic distortion induced by an illuminant channel. The strongly saturated green illuminant is the stationary state (−0.8, 0).
Fig. 7 The chromatic distortion induced by an opponency channel with α1,1 = 0.005, and α2,2 = 0.4.

Fig. 8 Level sets of relative entropy µ = 0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.04. For the blue ones t = 0, for the red ones t = (0.2, 0.9) (left). Level sets of relative entropy µ = 0.02 for t = (0, 0), (0.2, 0.9), (0.3, 0.3), (−0.3, −0.5), and (−0.6, 0.7) (right).
